texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium is an essential element, which is widely applied in lithium batteries [1,2], ceramic [3,4], communications [5], nuclear fusion power generation [6] and other fi elds. With the rapid development of electric vehicles, global consumption of lithium in 2019 was estimated to be about 57,700 tons of lithium content, which was increased by 18% from 49,100 tons of lithium content in 2018. And the proportion of batteries increased from 56% to 65% [7,8].

texts.orig: Generally, lithium resources are mainly distributed in Argentina, Chile, Australia, China, Bolivia and United States. Brines and minerals are the main resource for lithium recovery. In recent years, recovery of lithium from brines has been an important measure to ease the tension between supply and demand of lithium materials, because about 70 wt % of the lithium resources exists in brines. And it is more economical and environmental than extraction from minerals [9]. Table 1 shows the compositions of brines in Argentina, Chile, Bolivia, United States and China. The Mg/Li ratios of salt brines in Argentina, Chile and United States are much lower than those in Bolivia and China [10,11].

texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium is an essential material in the energy and electronics industry. As abundant lithium resources, lake brines are exploited largely to extract lithium. Selective electrodialysis (S-ED) is a booming technology to recover and separate lithium from brines with high Mg/Li mass ratio. The e ff ects of current, temperature and total dissolved solids (TDS) concentration of brine on the recovery rate of lithium, selective migration ratio, current e ffi ciency and energy consumption were investigated by single-stage S-ED processes. The results con fi rm that a high current below limiting current density (LCD) could increase both the recovery rate of lithium and separation performance. Under a high temperature, the ion separation of lithium and magnesium decreases obviously but the recovery rate of lithium changes barely. Reducing TDS concentration has almost no e ff ect on ion migration but increases energy consumption. Then according to the determination of LCD, a four-stage S-ED process with stepwise adjustment of current was conducted. The recovery rate of lithium could be obtained 90% with a decrease of Mg/Li mass ratio from 9.85 to 0.57.

The e ff ects of current, temperature and total dissolved solids (TDS) concentration of brine on the recovery rate of lithium, selective migration ratio, current e ffi ciency and energy consumption were investigated by single-stage S-ED processes. The results con fi rm that a high current below limiting current density (LCD) could increase both the recovery rate of lithium and separation performance. Under a high temperature, the ion separation of lithium and magnesium decreases obviously but the recovery rate of lithium changes barely. Reducing TDS concentration has almost no e ff ect on ion migration but increases energy consumption. Then according to the determination of LCD, a four-stage S-ED process with stepwise adjustment of current was conducted. Precipitation has been applied to separate and recover lithium from low Mg/Li ratio brines [11]. According to the suggestion of Yu et al. [13], the Mg/Li mass ratio of the high Mg/Li ratio brine is over 8. Using this method to treat high Mg/Li brines would consume a large amount of precipitant, so the cost is pretty high. Solvent extraction is applicable to separating lithium from high Mg/Li brines [14]. However, the corrosion of equipment by extractant and the residue of extractant in treated system are restrictions of the method. Adsorption method is e ff ective for extracting lithium from brine with low lithium concentration. In generally, the lithium concentration of many studied brine system ranges from 10 to 1000 mg/L [15 -18]. Therefore, a high-e ffi ciency but low-cost method for treating high Mg/Li brines with high lithium concentration is necessary to develop.

Hence, the selective electrodialysis (S-ED) equipped with these membranes has been applied to separate and extract lithium from brines, especially from the brines with high Mg/Li mass ratio [27,28]. Nie et al. [28,29] investigated lithium recovery from salt lake brines by S-ED. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many studies have been reported about the e ff ects of various process conditions on separation performance. Nie et al. [28] reported that lower temperature, higher fl ow rate and higher current density were bene fi cial to the fractionation of Li + and Mg 2+ from the brines with TDS concentration of 40 -240 g/L. After that, a natural brine process under 20 V of applied voltage was conducted [29]. The Mg/Li mass ratio was reduced to 2.07. Ji et al. [27] investigated the in fl uence of applied voltage and linear fl ow velocities on the extraction lithium. The TDS concentration of the brine was about 40 g/L. The Mg/Li mass ratio after S-ED process under the optimal conditions of 5 V of the applied voltage and 6.2 cm/s of the linear velocity was decreased to 7. Zhao et al. [30] investigated the lithium fraction from diverse binary cation systems at various operating temperatures by S-ED. The TDS concentration of studied systems was in the range of 45 -200 g/L. The results indicated that the recovery rate of lithium was obtained higher at higher temperature, and the separation coe ffi cients changed slightly. However, these studies only partially explained the e ff ects of these conditions on lithium recovery rate, ion selectivity and energy consumption. The comprehensive explanations of the e ff ects of factors are necessary. Meanwhile, the TDS concentration of the studied brine in this work is 408 g/L, much higher than the highest salinity of brines studied in the above study, 240 g/L [28]. It is necessary to compare the S-ED performance under di ff erent TDS concentrations of brines.

texts.orig: In this work, the e ff ect of applied current, operation temperature and brine salinity on the recovery of lithium, separation performance, current e ffi ciency and energy consumption were investigated by singlestage S-ED processes. The variation of these performances under different conditions were also discussed and explained. Then based on the determination of limiting current density (LCD), a multi-stage S-ED process with stepwise adjustment of current was conducted to obtain the product with a recovery rate of lithium over 90% and low Mg/Li mass ratio.

texts.orig: The commercial cation exchange membrane (CEM) and anion exchange membrane (AEM) used in this study were Selemion CSO and ASV (AGC Engineering CO., LTD.). Table 3 shows the detailed information about the membranes. CSO has a thin oppositely-charged surface layer, which could hinder the permeation of divalent cations by electrostatic repulsion [31,32]. For ASV, the monovalent anion selectivity comes from a highly crosslinked layer, sieving ions that possess di ff erent hydrated radius [33,34]. Since the high sulfate concentration (24.14 g/L) of brine in this study, the monovalent selective ASV was employed to avoid the scaling of CaSO4 on the membrane surface near the concentrate solution. All the membranes were cut into size of 16 × 28 cm 2 , consistent with the size required for the S-ED performance testing.

texts.orig: Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic diagram of S-ED apparatus. The experimental apparatus mainly consisted of anode solution chamber, concentrate solution chamber, dilute solution chamber, cathode solution chamber, membrane stack (AGC Engineering CO., LTD, CH-0), DC power (KORAD, KA3005D/P) and thermostatic water bath (NESLAB, RTE-111). The membrane stack contained 10 pairs of membranes with an e ff ective surface area of 0.021 m 2 . The spacer channel thickness was 0.75 mm. The membrane stack was connected to DC power through the indium-plated titanium (anode) and stainless steel (cathode) electrodes.

texts.orig: In this study, the e ff ects of applied current, temperature and brine salinity on S-ED performance were investigated using single-stage S-ED process. Before each experiment, the membranes were rinsed with DI water thoroughly for 24 h. When investigating the e ff ects of applied current and temperature, the evaporated brine was fi rst mixed with the same volume of water. Then 2.50 L of the diluted brine was added into the dilute solution chamber for each experiment. However, for the experiments of the di ff erent TDS concentration of brine, the brine was mixed with water at di ff erent dilution ratios of 1.0:0.5, 1.0:1.0, 1.0:1.5 (volume of brine: volume of deionized water). Then 2.50 L of undiluted brine and diluted brines with di ff erent salinity, as mentioned above, were added into the dilute solution chamber, respectively. The NaCl solution (2.50 L, 5%) was added to the concentrate solution chamber so that the voltage of power would not be too high under the constant current condition at the beginning of the experiments. The NaCl solution (2.00 L, 3%) was used as the cathode solution, and the pH value was adjusted below 7 by adding HCl during experiments since the alkali stability of membranes is poor. During the experiments, the strong oxidizing substances such as Cl2 could be generated on the anode

Furthermore, a blocking layer containing NaCl solution shared with cathode chamber was disposed between anode solution and membranes to hinder the contact of Cl2 and membranes. The pumps feed the solutions into the corresponding compartments with the fl owrate of 250 L/h for dilute and concentration solution. The linear velocity in the stack is 7.7 cm/s. The temperature was kept by the thermostatic water bath and coilers. All the experiments lasted 3 h. After each operation, the chambers, pipelines and membrane stack were cleaned by DI water for several times. The samples were taken every half an hour with recording the parameters such as volume, voltage and conductivity. The voltage of power and membrane stack were directly read from indicators on the power supply and a multimeter connected with the stack by two platinum wires, respectively. The solution properties during tests including pH value and conductivity were measured by a pH/conductivity meter (Mettler Toledo, S470-K SevenExcellence) with a pH electrode (Mettler Toledo, INLAB Expert Pro-ISM) and a conductivity electrode (Mettler Toledo, INLAB 731-ISM), respectively. The concentrations of Li + and Mg 2+ were both determined by ICP-OES (Spectro ARCOS FHS12).

texts.orig: In the dilute channel boundary layer, the ionic concentration varies steeply. And such concentration gradient becomes greater under higher applied current density [35]. Under a certain current density, the concentration at the membrane surface totally decreases to zero. Such current density is termed LCD. It is required to operate electrodialysis processes under a current density below LCD to avoid water dissociation [36] and further scaling of magnesium hydroxide [37]. Consequently, LCD is a critical parameter and its determination during S-ED experiments is required.
texts.text: In the dilute channel boundary layer, the ionic concentration varies steeply. And such concentration gradient becomes greater under higher applied current density [35]. Under a certain current density, the concentration at the membrane surface totally decreases to zero. Such current density is termed LCD. It is required to operate electrodialysis processes under a current density below LCD to avoid water dissociation [36] and further scaling of magnesium hydroxide [37]. Consequently, LCD is a critical parameter and its determination during S-ED experiments is required.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, the method proposed by Cowan and Brown [38] was used to determine the value of LCD in the plot of ∆ U/i against 1/i. After three-hour operation under the current of 1.3A, the measurement was conducted. The applied current was adjusted from 0 A to 3.2 A by an increment of 0.2 A each time. The values of current and the potential di ff erence of stack were recorded during the measurement. Fig. 2 shows the plot of the apparent resistance ∆ U/i against the reciprocal current density 1/i. The value of ∆ U/i initially decreases and then increases, so that an intersection of two extension lines of slopes is obtained. According to the intersection, the value of LCD could be determined and the corresponding current value is 2.06A, which is higher than 1.3A. Consequently, it is con fi rmed that the experiments were conducted under LCD without concentration polarization phenomenon.

texts.orig: Fig. 3(b) illustrates that the fl ux of Li + and Mg 2+ both increase with the increasing current and Li + is more a ff ected by the growth of current. The larger increase of lithium fl ux was consistent with the trend of the recovery rate. Therefore, the selective migration ratio increases with the growth of current in Fig. 3(c). These observations are consistent with the conclusions obtained by other authors. Nie et al. [28] studied the e ff ect of current density (5.9 -13.8 A/m 2 ) on fractionation of lithium ions from magnesium ions by S-ED. The selective coe ffi cient increased and Mg/Li mass ratio of product stream decreased with the growth of current density. At higher current density, more charge was carried by monovalent ions. Ji et al. [27] conducted S-ED experiments on recovering lithium from high Mg/Li ratio brines under various applied voltage. The migration rate of lithium showed a positive relationship with the voltage, so the separation coe ffi cient was higher under higher applied voltage. Galama et al. [42] investigated the e ff ect of current density (10 -300 A/m 2 ) on the desalination of ternary salt mixture with a lab scale ED stack. The selective removal of divalent ions increased at lower current density. Concentration polarization phenomenon occurs between the bulk solution and membrane surface. Because of the di ff erences in the transport numbers of ions in the solution and in the membrane, a concentration gradient is established in such region, which is also known as di ff usion boundary layer (DBL) [21,43]. No convection occurs and mass transfer is controlled by diffusion-migration in the thin stagnant layer. Roghmans et al. [44] investigated the permselectivity of cation-exchange membranes bearing an ion selective coating. As a consequence, ions could transport across the membrane more smoothly.

The current e ffi ciency decreases with increasing temperature in the range of 293.15 K to 303.15 K. As said before, low temperature could promote migration of monovalent ions with small hydrated ionic radius and high temperature could enhance the transport of divalent ions. Thus, the migration of divalent ions, especially Mg 2+ , is enhanced and contributes more to the current at high temperature. Consequently, the current e ffi ciency of lithium declines. Moreover, the speci fi c energy consumption decreases with the increase of temperature. The total voltage increases at low temperature due to the lower conductivity of solution.

texts.orig: It can be concluded that decreasing the TDS concentration of brine a ff ects barely on the ion transport across membrane, as the fl ux changes little. The energy consumption increases due to the change of conductivity. Some studies [50,51] have been reported that the thin positive charged layer on one side surface of CSO membrane might be detached during long-term operation. Therefore, the performance stability, especially monovalent selectivity, of membranes in long-term immersion of high salinity brine is under study to simulate continuous operation of industrial S-ED process. It can be expected that the monovalent selectivity of CSO will decline in a long-term operation, and maybe the rate of decline is related to brine salinity.

texts.orig: Based on the above results, the recovery rate of lithium and separation performance between lithium and magnesium both exhibit greater under a high current. However, under high current, the concentration gradient in boundary layer is great. The concentration of lithium at the membrane surface may decrease to zero due to the decreasing concentration of lithium in bulk solution. Thus, the value of LCD decreases and the current is adjusted to reduce the current gradient and avoid concentration polarization phenomenon. In general, the current density is adjusted in the range of 60% to 80% of the determined value of LCD. As the value of LCD continues to decrease, the current is reduced stepwise. Therefore, a multi-stage S-ED process is conducted.

The increasing temperature allows the reduction of a ffi nity between ions and water molecules and the enhancement swelling behavior of polymer chains of membrane. The radius of partially dehydrated ions gets smaller and the pore radius of membrane becomes larger. So that the promotion of magnesium ion transport exhibits more obvious. Considering the signi fi cant increase of energy consumption and slight decrease of lithium recovery under low temperature, temperature range of 293.15 K to 298.15 K is appropriate. texts.orig: Based on the results, a multi-stage S-ED process was conducted to recover over 90% of lithium. The current density was adjusted in the range of 60% to 80% of the value of LCD determined every 1 h. Due to the decrease of LCD, the current was reduced stepwise and varied into four stages. The lithium recovery of 90% could be achieved with Mg/Li mass ratio being reduced to 0.57. The leakage rate of sulfate ions is only 1.45%. Besides, further recovering would consume a lot of energy and Mg/Li mass ratio would increase to 0.88.
